segments.title: Astria — Face Inpainting (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — Face Inpainting

segments.title: Before / after — Final vs Original
segments.script:
Start with a few existing lookbook examples.

Original is the first Nano Banana image. Final is after Inpaint faces runs a
second Nano Banana pass over the detected face area. No separate model, and
no wardrobe or lighting story here. We are checking the face itself.

If the original turns smooth or glassy and the final keeps pores, lashes,
pigmentation, and eye detail, that is the cost earning its keep.

The best result should also preserve identity. It should refine the face from
the same identity reference, not push the person into a generic
skin-enhanced look.

segments.title: Why Face Inpainting matters
segments.script:
Now why it matters.

Use Face Inpainting when identity has to survive the final crop: lookbook
pages, product detail zoom, campaign crops, or client review.

It is not a separate model. It is a second Nano Banana pass over the
detected face area.

Unlike a generic skin enhancer, it works with the identity reference, so the
goal is the same person with better pores, pigmentation, lashes, and eye
detail.

Budget extra processing time and roughly double generation work.

segments.title: Workflow — 4K + Inpaint faces
segments.script:
Workflow inside Astria.

Start with a simple lookbook brief. We care about Nano Banana, 4K, and the
face pass.

Set resolution to 4K. Open the cog wheel and turn on Inpaint faces.

When you run it, Astria creates the image, then runs a second Nano Banana
pass over the detected face area. That is the extra cost and time.

For the comparison, open a completed result, pan into the face, and compare
Final with Original.

segments.title: FAQs
segments.script:
Rule: Inpaint faces is not a separate model. It is a second Nano Banana pass
over the detected face area.

It should not smooth everyone into the same face. Because the generation uses
an identity reference, the face pass should preserve the person while
improving detail.

Use it for inspected faces, budget extra time and double generation work, and
review Final against Original at 4K before shipping.

segments.title: Astria — Artboard (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — Artboard

segments.title: Create a cinematic fashion video
segments.script:
Today we are going to create a cinematic fashion video with fast-paced
shots, consistent characters, and real wardrobe references.

The goal is not one random clip. The goal is a repeatable workflow:
start from references, plan the shots with an artboard, then use that
same artboard to guide video.

We will make a first version, then edit the atmosphere into a darker
outdoor mood with greenery.

segments.title: Artboards are the shot plan
segments.script:
To get there, we use artboards created by ChatGPT in a single image.
Each artboard is a four by four grid. Sixteen frames, one image.

This gives us a quick preview of the movie before we generate the
video. We can check the cast, the wardrobe, the location, the mood,
and the camera variety.

For beginners, this is the important idea: the artboard is your shot
plan. It tells the video model what world it should preserve.

segments.title: Review the workspace references
segments.script:
Let's get to work. Start in the workspace that already has the product
templates and references you want to use.

First check the cast. These are the people the video should keep
consistent from shot to shot.

Then check the wardrobe. These garment references are the clothes we
want visible across the final film.

When you write the prompt, mention the references in plain language:
the girl, the woman, the man, the shirt, the pants, and the jacket.

segments.title: Ask the assistant for an artboard
segments.script:
Open Astria Assistant and type slash artboard. Then describe the film
in normal words.

Ask for an editorial fashion video on a white studio background. Name
every character and the exact garment that belongs to them, using the
template references as the source of truth.

Be explicit that looks and outfits should not swap. The girl stays in
her template outfit, the woman stays in hers, and the man keeps his
own jacket, pants, and shoes.

Choose sixteen by nine. The assistant expands your short brief into a
detailed artboard prompt, with a mix of wide shots, medium shots,
close-ups, and product details.

That generated prompt is what creates the first artboard.

segments.title: Review the first artboard tiles
segments.script:
Once the artboard is ready, open it and review the tiles before you
generate video.

A good fashion video needs camera variety. Look for long shots that
establish the full group, medium shots that show wardrobe, close-ups
for emotion, and detail shots for fast cutaways.

If the artboard already has that range, it is ready to become a video
reference.

segments.title: Generate video from the artboard
segments.script:
When you are happy with the artboard, use it to generate video.

Copy the same artboard prompt into the Video tab, but remove the word
artboard. Attach the artboard image as a reference image, not as a
first frame.

Choose Seedance2 Fast, either 480p or 720p, set the duration to
fifteen seconds, and generate.

segments.title: Review the first video
segments.script:
Now review the video. Do not only ask if it looks cool. Ask if it
follows the artboard.

Check whether the same characters and garments stay visible, whether
the cuts feel fast enough, and whether the shots still feel like one
campaign.

segments.title: Refine the atmosphere
segments.script:
To edit the direction, stay in the same chat session. Do not click new
chat, and do not start the workflow over.

Ask for one clear change: make the same fashion video more moody,
darker, outdoor, and surrounded by greenery. Also remind the assistant
to keep the same characters in their original template garments.

Because you are still in the same conversation, the assistant keeps
the prior context, writes a new artboard prompt, and creates a refined
grid. This is the loop: create, inspect, then ask for a focused change.

segments.title: Review the moody artboard
segments.script:
Review the refined artboard the same way. Look for the new mood, but
also check that the original references did not disappear.

The best refinement changes the atmosphere without losing the people,
the clothes, or the shot variety.

segments.title: Review the moody video
segments.script:
Generate the second video from the refined board and review it the
same way.

The new clip should feel darker and more atmospheric, but still match
the references. The artboard is how we keep the model from drifting
away from the campaign.

segments.title: Closing tips
segments.script:
Here are the rules to remember.

First, name the references. Tell the assistant which cast, garments,
and location should appear.

Second, ask for camera variety. A fast video needs wide shots, medium
shots, close-ups, and details.

Third, refine in the same chat. Do not rebuild from scratch. Ask for
one change, review the new artboard, and then generate the next video.

segments.title: Astria — Artboard (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — Artboard

segments.title: What the artboard skill makes
segments.script:
This is an artboard: one image that plans a whole short film.
It is a four-by-four grid — sixteen cinematic frames — so you can
see the story before you spend time animating it.

For a fashion campaign, that means one cast, one wardrobe, one
location, and one look across every frame. First you make the
artboard. Then you use it as the visual plan for video. These two
examples both started from the same Astria workspace.

segments.title: Prepare the workspace
segments.script:
First, prepare the workspace. Start in Astria with a workspace that
already has product templates. Here, America Basics has a shirt, a
jacket, and a pair of pants. Each one is a reusable photoshoot setup.

Before you ask for an artboard, check the reference library. Click
References, then use Filters. Filter to Human. These are the people
your film can use: the girl, the woman, and the man.

Now filter to Garment. This is the wardrobe: jackets, shirts,
pants, and shoes. The beginner idea is simple: cast plus wardrobe is
the raw material for the artboard.

Open a template and look at a finished prompt. You are not copying
it. You are learning the shape of a good request: who is in the
shot, what they wear, where they are, and what style the image has.

Check the other templates the same way. The jacket uses the man. The
pants use the woman and a closer product detail. Three templates,
one shared cast and wardrobe.

Once you can see those ingredients, the workspace is ready. In the
next step, you will open the assistant and turn this into a
sixteen-shot artboard.

segments.title: Generate the artboard
segments.script:
Now make the artboard. Open Astria Assistant and start a fresh chat.
This is the point where you stop clicking around and describe the film.

Type slash artboard. Then write the brief in plain language. Use the
same simple recipe every time: cast, wardrobe, location, mood, and
aspect ratio.

For this first version, ask for all three models together on a clean
off-white studio backdrop, calm and editorial, with the full wardrobe
visible across the grid. This is the starting board, not the final
mood pass.

Send it. If the assistant asks for an aspect ratio, choose sixteen by
nine for a horizontal campaign video. Choose nine by sixteen only
when you are planning a vertical cut.

The assistant expands your short brief into a sixteen-shot plan. Look
for variety: wide shots, medium shots, close-ups, product details,
and group shots. That is what makes the video pass feel edited.

Stop here and let the first board finish. The next step is review:
open the generated artboard in the lightbox and check whether the
first version matches the brief.

segments.title: Review the first artboard
segments.script:
When the first artboard finishes, open it from the newest prompt. Use
the lightbox so you can see the whole four-by-four grid at once.

This first version is the clean studio board from the initial prompt.
It should show the three models, the wardrobe, and a mix of shot
types: portraits, full-body frames, group shots, and product details.

Review it like a checklist. Are the right people there? Is the
wardrobe visible? Does the location match the brief? Is there enough
variety for a video editor to cut with?

If the answer is almost yes, do not rebuild from scratch. Keep the
same chat open and ask for one clear change. In the next step, we turn
this clean board into a moodier creative direction.

segments.title: Refine the artboard
segments.script:
Now refine the board in the same chat. Do not start over and do not
paste the whole brief again. Keep the context, and ask for the one
change that matters.

Here the clean studio version works, but the campaign needs more
attitude. Type slash artboard and ask for it to be more moody, darker,
and surrounded by greenery.

The assistant keeps the cast and the structure, then writes a new
prompt for the updated treatment. You are changing the direction, not
rebuilding the workflow.

When the new result lands in Prompts, open it in the lightbox. Now the
grid has a darker, more cinematic look, while still behaving like the
same storyboard.

That is the repeatable loop: create the first board, inspect it, then
refine in the same chat with one clear request.

segments.title: Switch the artboard to video
segments.script:
Now turn the artboard into video. Stay on the same prompts page, but
switch from Image to Video.

Attach the artboard as a reference image. It is not the first frame.
It is the visual plan: cast, wardrobe, framing, and mood.

For the text, reuse the same idea from the image prompt, but remove
the word artboard. Now ask for the actual film: a short America Basics
fashion video with the same cast, wardrobe, location, mood, and
sixteen-by-nine format.

Choose Seedance2 Fast 720p, keep the duration short, and generate.
Then move on. Do not make the viewer watch the render wait.

segments.title: Review the generated videos
segments.script:
After the render is done, review the videos against the artboard. Do
not judge only whether the clip looks cool. Check whether it follows
the plan.

On the first pass, look for the opening energy, the walking shots,
the same court, and the same wardrobe. The artboard tiles give you
the shot list.

Now compare the second pass. It can make different motion choices,
but it should still feel like the same campaign: same cast, same
wardrobe, same location, same warm athletic mood.

This is the handoff: the artboard is the director's reference, and the
video prompt tells the model how those frames should move.

segments.title: FAQs
segments.script:
Three beginner rules make an artboard work.

First, keep the world consistent. Use the same cast, the same
wardrobe, one clear location, and one mood across the whole grid.

Second, vary the shot sizes. Ask for wide shots, medium shots,
close-ups, and product details. That gives the video pass something
to cut between.

Third, choose the aspect ratio before you generate. Use sixteen by
nine for horizontal, or nine by sixteen for vertical. Match the
artboard to the final video you want.

segments.title: Astria — Edit (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — Edit

segments.title: Two clean-input edits
segments.script:
Astria Edit isn't a creative tool. It's a prep tool. Two examples.
On the left — an editorial shot with the shoes stripped out. On the
right — a phone snapshot of a bag, cleaned into a studio packshot.
Same image, minus the noise. Ready for whatever you generate next.

segments.title: Strip the styling
segments.script:
Edit is for cleaning inputs. Watch.

Drop an image into Astria. Here — a fashion shot, woman on a pink
chair, black heels.

Type one sentence. Remove the shoes. Keep her barefoot. Preserve
everything else.

Hit generate.

Same pose, same dress, same chair, same light — shoes gone. This is
what makes a reference clean. When you train a face or generate from
this image later, the heels won't travel with it.

segments.title: One-sentence packshot cleanup
segments.script:
Same tool, different job.

Here's a phone snapshot of a bag — kitchen table, keys, receipts,
harsh flash. Now we want a clean studio packshot.

The full packshot pack we showed you last video runs to two hundred
lines of prompt. With Edit, you can say it in one sentence — clean
studio packshot, plain background, flat lighting, no shadows.

Same bag, same color, same logo. Production-ready in one shot.

segments.title: FAQs
segments.script:
Three things worth knowing.

Surgical, not creative. Edit's strongest move is removing or
replacing one thing — a pair of shoes, a kitchen table, a logo.
Don't ask it to reimagine the whole image.

Minimal prompts win. Say what changes. Remove X, keep everything
else exact. The longer the prompt, the more the model has to ignore.

Use it as a prep step. Strip styling before training a face.
Clean references before generating packshots. Isolate subjects
before video. Edit is the first link in the chain — the rest of
Astria starts working better when the inputs are clean.

segments.title: Astria — 3D Packshots (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — 3D Packshots

segments.title: How simple — one photo in, a campaign out
segments.script:
Here's the whole workflow. You upload one product still. It can be
a polished studio shot or a phone snap on the cutting table.
Astria returns a clean 360° rotation plus a sheet of multi-angle
stills, ready to drop into the product page, the lookbook, the
Reels grid. No light kit. No turntable. No retoucher on standby.
One image in. An editorial-grade campaign out.

segments.title: Workflow — upload one photo, get a packshot
segments.script:
Here's the whole flow inside Astria.

Open the 3D Packshot template. Every pack has a built-in upload, so
the product image goes in and the packshot comes out.

Upload a single product image. A clean studio shot is ideal, but
even a phone shot works. The cleaner the source, the more faithful
the rotation.

Click Generate. The queue picks it up, and a few moments later you
have a full 3D rotation plus a set of high-quality stills, ready
to drop into a product page, a lookbook, or a Reels grid.

segments.title: Before / after — phone snap vs clean packshot
segments.script:
You don't need a studio. This backpack on the left was shot on a
cluttered kitchen table at midnight, harsh phone flash, coffee ring
on the wood. Basically the worst possible reference. Astria takes
that single ugly snapshot and turns it into the clean studio rotation
on the right. Same backpack, same straps, same hardware, production
quality without the production.

segments.title: Why it matters — the conversion math
segments.script:
So why does this matter?

Better product visuals help shoppers understand the product faster,
trust what they see, and feel more confident buying online.

360-degree product views have been linked to higher conversions,
stronger engagement, and fewer returns across ecommerce case
studies.

Practical Ecommerce reports that 360-degree product photography
has been associated with sales lifts ranging from 5 to 40 percent.
One cited study suggests a 14 percent increase in sales and a
17 percent better shopping experience.

In another example, True Value reportedly saw a 22 percent
conversion lift after adding 360-degree product photography, while
Home Depot saw fewer returns during a 360-degree product-photo
A/B test.

segments.title: FAQs
segments.script:
Three things to know.

Reference image. Any clean product shot works. A studio capture is
ideal, but a clear phone photo can work too. The cleaner the source,
the more faithful the rotation.

Format and finish. Studio-quality three-by-four visuals, ready for
PDPs, lookbooks, and Reels.

Production efficiency. Much more affordable than a traditional
product shoot, so you can test backdrops, angles, and styling
options before locking the final visuals.

segments.title: Astria — Video Style Transfer (TV intro)
segments.script: (visual segment) ASTRIA — Video Style Transfer

segments.title: Result showcase
segments.script:
Here is what you can build with Astria's video style transfer.
Two finished clips, both driven by the same reference video,
each styled with a single prompt.

segments.title: Four-step workflow
segments.script:
Here's the whole flow inside Astria.

Step one. Build the image with your own assets. Clothes, shoes,
models, jewelry. Drop in everything you want the video to feature.

Step two. Upload a reference video and a single simple prompt.
The reference is your motion source. The prompt is your style
direction. Both fit in a few short lines.

Step three. Pick the resolution. Four-eighty for a fast preview,
seven-twenty for the daily driver, ten-eighty for the final cut.
Your call.

That's it. A few minutes later the finished video lands, with your
style and your garments, ready to drop into a campaign.

segments.title: First-frame match
segments.script:
Here's the trick. The model locks the look from the driving video's
first frame: the face, the pose, the garments, the background. So
before you spend credits on a full video, generate just the still
in the image tab. Iterate the prompt until the first frame matches
what you want. Then switch to video mode and render. Way faster,
way cheaper.

segments.title: FAQs
segments.script:
Two more things worth knowing.

Each clip runs up to fifteen seconds. For anything longer, just
concat a handful together into one continuous cut.

And the prompt is your director. Shape the motion you want, the
background, the lighting, the camera angle, the mood. Specific
phrases like natural relaxed pose, fumbling her eyes, or
micro-expressions help the model land the exact look you want.
The more you direct, the closer the result.

segments.title: Scaling AI photoshoots
segments.script:
Thanks for having me. Today we're going to cover how to scale up lookbooks,
editorials, social content, and campaigns for fashion, e-commerce, and
direct-to-consumer brands. The goal is to build a methodic system that will
allow us to repeatably and consistently simulate an on-brand photoshoot day:
to bring up all the creative knowledge, brand requirement and implement the 
brief designs into an automated system.

segments.title: Outline — what we'll cover today
segments.script:
Let me quickly outline what we'll cover today. First, we will review the current state of 
traditional photoshoots, and how things look today.

Then we will see what AI photoshoots look like today, and see few examples for
major brands who have implemented AI. 

Then we will dive into brand identity and how we can translate it into AI: how to apply
it, how to work as a creative director inside Astria, and how to use
real-world references. 

From there we'll get into prompt editing and
refinement — making sure you consistently get strong outputs with fewer
bugs and artifacts. 

Once stable and ready, we will templatize our work so that
you can run looks faster — that's the core of the "scale up." 

We'll also
cover the Describe tool for bringing existing client assets into the AI
world.

Finally we will deliver a digital product to a client: not just images,
but a template the brand's studio can run themselves.

segments.title: Traditional photoshoot
segments.script:
Let's start by reviewing how do these currently work pre-AI?
What does does traditional photoshoot look like?
Usually this would involve logistics such as
coordinating models, locations, shipping inventory, making sure product arrives from production to the studio on time.

Stylists, makeup, hair — all of this logistics exists, ultimately, to serve the brand's DNA.
The entire photoshoot is there to convey the brand's identity,
and we need to somehow implement and communicate that. Now let's see how we can do that with AI.

segments.title: Who this is for — brands & verticals
segments.script:
Before we go further, let me clarify who this is actually for.

On the brand side, we work with three types of business.
E-commerce — companies selling product online. DTC brands —
going directly to the consumer, with or without a store. And
traditional retailers who need scalable visual content for
their catalogs.

The verticals where this really shines are fashion and apparel,
footwear, outdoor and professional gear, jewelry, glasses, and
beauty. If your product lives on a model or on a body, AI
photoshoots are going to change how you produce imagery.

segments.title: Real brands already using AI
segments.script:
Let's see a few examples of brands already using AI.

SACKS rebuilt their entire homepage. The hero imagery,
the model shots, the product page lookbooks — all done by the professional photographer 
Shai Yechezkel & Miki Turgeman who have formed an AI studio called CI898. Shai and Miki helped
steer the brand's creative direction together, built
the brand's avatars and template set, and then ran hundreds of looks through it.
They're already done two collections with AI.

Ronny Kobo is another example of a Paris based designer. You can see the difference in styling
and how the branding gets applied translated into the visuals.

Finally the big players such as Mango, H&M, Zara — are all doing the same
thing, producing both campaigns and lookbooks with AI. The world is
already implementing AI. What remains is to direct it well, scale it
up, and do it precisely and custom to each brand's identity.

segments.title: Brand identity in Astria — three real implementations
segments.script:
Here's how we actually implement brand identity in Astria.

Every brand has its own visual language — its own hex colors, its own
models, expressions, and atmosphere. Off-the-shelf templates don't
work because a brand will always say "this is beautiful, but it's
not me."

So we build a workspace for each brand, with their models,
backgrounds, lighting, and template set defined once — and reused
across every collection.

Here is an example of fashion brand modeled after Zara — editorial fashion, outdoor shoots, European aesthetic.
A set of templates across casual, formal, and nightwear.

Great Sports — Adidas-style. Higher intensity expressions, athletic
energy, a completely different set of models and templates.

And America Basics — a Uniqlo-style brand. Soft neutrals, friendly
expressions. Clean, everyday. Completely different DNA from the
other two.

For each brand we deliver a workspace with a set of templates — one
per garment type. The studio uploads a product and gets the full
lookbook in a single click.

segments.title: The platform is the partner
segments.script:
Here's what we've learned working with brands: they need constant
help keeping up with AI. Brands are busy with their core business: design, logistics, fashion. 
They do not have time to write prompts, and they don't need to.

That's exactly what we're trying to solve. Six months ago we used Flux LoRA. 
Then it was Nano Banana. Today it's Nano Banana 2. In two
months it will be Nano Banana 3, Seedance or WAN 2.7.
The brand doesn't need to know.

The brand receives a template. The brand's studio can then
upload a simple packshot photo, and get a full lookbook in a
single click. The framework was defined for them: the models, the
lighting, the framing, and so now they can scale it up and run looks in bulks.

Sometimes the brand may way to do small customizations. The Brief box allow to specify a customization
like "this time she's sitting on an armchair," or "make her pose more dynamic" —
and it runs the entire template which keeps results consistent and tell a coherent story.

Finally, it's important to explain that brands need a set of templates — one for jackets, one for
shoes, one for outdoor shots. Each garment type needs different
crops, framing and poses. The brand can then pick a template, and upload a bulk of 
garments to run the lookbook in a single click.

segments.title: Your turn — start creating
segments.script:
Alright — your turn.

First thing, let's open a Workspace. Think of it as a shared
room — your team, creatives, CMOs, photographers, all working
side by side. Every prompt, reference, and template you create
shows up live for everyone in there.

To set one up, click the Workspace selector at the top-left,
next to the logo, and pick "New workspace". Name it after
the brand you're working with, and hit "Create".

Now — pick a brand you actually love. Skip the made-up exercise
and grab something real. A designer you admire, a brand you've
worked with, maybe something local you've been watching.

Pull up their current material. Look at the poses, the faces,
the hair, the makeup, the expressions — what makes the look
theirs.

Let's get our hands dirty.

segments.title: Open a Workspace for the brand
segments.script:
Let's start by opening a new workspace. Click the Workspace selector
next to the logo, pick "New workspace", and name it after the brand
you're working with. I'll name mine Bobo Choses.

segments.title: Brand research — what makes the brand recognizable
segments.script:
Here's the Bobo Choses website. Let's think about the brand's
identity. How does the lookbook look — white studio, gray, concrete
floor, or outdoor? How do the models look — age group, expression,
hair? And the poses — strict front, back, 45-side, detail view, or
something more dynamic?

The adult faces have a neutral expression while the kids look a
little brighter. The women's hair is parted, the skin is pale.
Adult ages are around 22 to 26, kids around 8.

Now let's examine the lookbook itself. On the kids' product page, a
medium upper-torso waist-up view with very little headroom. The
full-body view leaves a lot of whitespace, with socks and flip-flops,
45-degree front, hands relaxed at the side.

The women's lookbook follows the same pattern — medium shot, upper
torso, head cropped, waist up. The full-body view has plenty of
whitespace, 45 degrees, legs in a V, hands at the side. And a
dynamic shot rotated, with hands behind the head.

The aspect ratio across the lookbook is 3:4 — quite common.

Now let's go back to Astria and simulate a photoshoot day. We'll try
a kids lookbook — one of the main pain points for brands, and where
they're usually happy to start migrating to AI. For starters, let's
get a baseline image to get things going.

segments.title: Technicalities — model, resolution, aspect, count
segments.script:
Now for a few technicalities.

Model — make sure Nano Banana 2 is selected.

Resolution — 2K for iterating quickly. Once you're happy with the
look, switch to 4K.

Aspect ratio — 3:4. Most lookbooks are 3:4.

And for quicker iteration, two images per prompt. Later, bump it to
four if you want more variation.

segments.title: Build the look — Lookbook references one cube at a time
segments.script:
Let's click the Lookbook button and start adding references. We'll
go through each cube — one at a time.

Background.

The Background cube has a color picker built in. I'll pick white —
clean studio look, good for product images.

Pose.

Click the Pose cube and the picker opens. You'll see options like
back view, front pose, full body, 45 angle, back medium, front
medium. I'll go with Front medium — straightforward, waist up,
classic catalog framing.

Face.

Click the Face cube. Same picker pattern, but here we get the
public library — every face was generated by Astria and is free to
use. I'll search for "boy" and watch how the list filters down.
Felix looks right for our look.

Top.

For garments, the fastest path is to copy a packshot straight from
the brand's site. Right-click copy, then back to Astria, focus the
prompt box, and Command-V. The image uploads instantly and lands in
the Top cube.

Bottom.

Same flow with the shorts.

Footwear.

The public library has shoes too. I'll search for "flip" — and
there's our Minimalist black flip-flops. Click, and it's added.

segments.title: Refine prompts, describe, templatize
segments.script:
Most bugs you'll hit aren't the model being wrong — they're conflicts
between the crop you asked for and the references you loaded. A medium
shot with a shoe reference tries to cram a face and shoes into one
frame and distorts. Keep the crop, the references, and the prompt
telling the same story.

When you need finer control than the Lookbook gives you, go to the
prompt box. A handful of high-impact keywords goes a long way —
diffused lighting, realistic skin, soft expression. That's where brand
DNA lives: serious or pleased, high-end or low-cost.

The Describe tool closes the loop between references and prompts. Let
me grab a reference from Unsplash — a search for fashion photography
pulls scenes close to what we want. Copy one, back to Astria, click
Paste from clipboard, then Describe. The tool extracts a prompt, but
it listens to the references you already have and avoids describing
them. No more white shirt in the prompt fighting a yellow dress in
the reference.

Once a look works — model, pose, lighting, expression — stop iterating
and templatize. Turn the pack into a template. From there it's a
digital product: the brand's studio picks a template, uploads a
packshot, and runs the lookbook. Build once, scale forever.

segments.title: Deliver the template — the brand runs at scale
segments.script:
Once a look is working — the model, the background, the lighting,
the framing — we stop iterating and lock it in. Open the hamburger
on a generation, click Template, and save a New Template. From that
point, no matter what garment you drop in, it will keep the model,
the background, and the lighting you originally defined.

This template is what we deliver to the brand. It's not a folder of
images; it's a recipe. The brand opens My Templates, picks the one
we built for them, swaps in a new garment — and everything else stays
consistent: the same angle, the same model, the same scene.

The template itself holds a collection of prompts — front, side, back,
detail. That's what makes this scale. A brand gets a hundred shirts
from the manufacturer; they select thirty at once, click Generate,
and the template runs them across four models in the same angles
and the same look. That's the end of the workflow: we build the
template, the brand runs the lookbook.

segments.title: Wrap-up
segments.script:
The short version: train the brand, ship the template, let the brand
run the looks. Creatives stay in the directing chair — the template
does the cranking. If that model fits what you're trying to build,
start at astria.ai. Thanks for listening.
